data.title: Abbott Laboratories: Buy This Dividend King On Sale Now
data.description:
This article was written by Kody Kester (Kody's Dividends).

As a long-term investor, I believe it's important to take the long view and remain calm amid panic when a company's fundamentals are sound. This is what makes earnings season so fascinating to me.

You never know whose earnings report is going to spark panic among "investors" and finicky trading algorithms. It's also difficult to predict what will freak the market out on a stock.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT) will probably go down as one of the bigger post-earnings slumps of this earnings season. Today, it slumped 10% with the release of its fourth-quarter earnings. Q1 2026 midpoint adjusted EPS guidance of $1.15 ($1.1 to $1.18) was just below the consensus of $1.20. For the full year 2026, though, guidance was largely on point.

When I last covered ABT with a Buy rating in January 2025, I thought it could realistically deliver double-digit percentage annual adjusted diluted EPS over the next few years. The company's AA-rated balance sheet was another positive. Lastly, shares were slightly discounted.

One calendar year later, I'm reaffirming my Buy rating. Despite the noise of weakness from the Nutrition and Diagnostics segments, ABT's core products (FreeStyle Libre, the Volt PFA System, etc.) continued to perform well. The company's pending acquisition of Exact Sciences is another reason for optimism. ABT's interest coverage ratio points to a financially sound business. Clinching my buy case is the fact that shares are now deeply undervalued.

Abbott is Revving Up the Growth Engine

As I alluded to earlier, earlier today, ABT shared its financial results for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company's reported net sales grew by 3.8% year-over-year to $11.46 billion during the quarter. This came up $330 million shy of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter.

Factoring in foreign currency translation tailwinds to the tune of 1.4%, ABT's organic net sales increased by 3% over the year-ago period for the fourth quarter. On the other side of the equation, COVID-19 testing net sales hurt the company's net sales to the tune of 0.8% during the quarter. Overall, organic net sales excluding COVID-19 tests edged 3.8% higher year-over-year in the quarter.

So, what elements contributed to ABT's topline miss for the fourth quarter? The Nutrition segment was the leading cause behind the company's net sales miss during the quarter. Its reported net sales declined 8.9% (-9.1% organically) over the year-ago period to $1.94 billion in the quarter. As Chairman and CEO Robert Ford put it in his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call, this was driven by volume challenges. Raising prices in recent years to keep up with surging commodity costs has suppressed consumer demand as consumers have become more price sensitive. These issues were most pronounced in the Pediatric business, with U.S. net sales tailing off 17.7% to $463 million.

The secondary pressure point for ABT for the fourth quarter came from its Diagnostics segment. The segment's reported net sales decreased by 2.5% year-over-year (-3.6% organically) to $2.46 billion during the quarter. COVID-19 testing-related sales were nearly cut in half to $89 million in the quarter, which is to be expected as the final remnants of the COVID-19 testing revenue wash out over time. Backing out COVID-19 testing headwinds, the segment's net sales were relatively flat, down 0.2%.

On the flip side, ABT displayed tremendous strength in its Medical Devices and Established Pharmaceuticals segments for the fourth quarter. The former's net sales jumped 12.3% (+10.4% organically) over the year-ago period to $5.68 billion during the quarter. Momentum from top products like the FreeStyle Libre continuous glucose monitor franchise and the Aveir leadless pacemaker was to credit for this growth in the quarter. As was the new product launch of the Volt PFA System in the U.S. (ABT's first PFA offering in the U.S.).

The Established Pharmaceuticals segment reported a 9.0% surge (+7.0% organically) in net sales to $1.38 billion for the fourth quarter. That was made possible by double-digit growth in India and several countries across Latin America and the Middle East.

ABT's adjusted diluted EPS vaulted 11.9% higher year-over-year to $1.50 during the fourth quarter. This came in $0.01 above the analyst consensus in the quarter.

For full-year 2026, ABT anticipates midpoint organic net sales growth of 7% (6.5% to 7.5%). The company's midpoint adjusted diluted EPS guidance of $5.675 would represent 10% growth.

As FreeStyle Libre CGMs continue to increase their penetration, this is one growth catalyst for ABT. Another growth driver for the company is the recent launch of the Volt PFA System in the U.S. Currently, there are around 12 million people in the U.S. with AFib. Over the next 20 years, this is expected to double. The combination of this being a minimally invasive procedure with high efficacy should sustain strong growth for this product as well.

ABT also recognizes that it's going to have to play the volume game with its Nutrition segment if it hopes to revitalize growth there. In the fourth quarter, the company began implementing price and promotion initiatives to kickstart that process. In the meantime, the company expects to launch at least eight new products over the next 12 months to breathe new life into the segment.

Finally, ABT announced a couple of months ago that it was acquiring Exact Sciences (EXAS) in a $21 billion deal. This will add the market-leading noninvasive colorectal cancer screening option (Cologuard), the personalized treatment decision technology (Oncotype), and the multi-cancer early detection blood test (Cancerguard) products to its portfolio. The deal is expected to close in Q2 2026 and would add complementary, high-growth products to its portfolio.

These factors have me cautiously optimistic that ABT can deliver 10.5% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth through 2028, off a 2025 base of $5.15.

ABT is also financially stable. The company's interest coverage ratio in 2025 was 43.5. This supports an AA- S&P credit rating with a negative outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to ABT's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and ABT's Q4 2025 Infographic).

Fair Value is Approaching $135 a Share

In the last 12 months, ABT's shares have returned -7%. That's a stark contrast to the 15% total return posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time. This happened as ABT's adjusted diluted EPS has grown, and I'm now incorporating 2026 and 2027 forecasts into my forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input. That has taken shares from a slight discount a year ago to a much more pronounced undervaluation today.

At the current $108 share price (as of January 22nd, 2026), ABT is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 19. This is well below the FAST Graphs 10-year average P/E ratio (and my fair value estimate) of 23.6.

Why do I still believe that ABT deserves such a valuation multiple? Well, the company's 10.5% annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is well above the 10-year CAGR of 7.4%. This suggests ABT's growth profile is intact.

Currently, the calendar year 2026 is 6% in the books. That leaves another 94% of 2026 and 6% of 2027 yet to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $5.71.

Plugging that into my fair value P/E ratio of 23.6, I compute a fair value of $134 a share. Relative to the current share price, that equates to a 20% discount to fair value.

If ABT reverts to fair value and lives up to growth expectations, it could have a 13% upside by the end of 2026 (not including the 10% drop today). Through 2030, ABT could generate 12% annual total returns (also not accounting for the selloff today).

The Payout Can Keep Climbing

ABT's 2.3% forward dividend yield comes in meaningfully above the healthcare sector's 1.3% median forward yield. This represents a 60 basis point widening of the yield spread versus last January. That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System issues B and C+ grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (the former of which is up from a B- grade last January). These grades also don't yet consider the selloff today, which could incrementally improve them.

ABT is just as much of an income growth story as it is a starting income story, too. The company has grown its payout for 54 consecutive years (though it shows up as 12 years in the Quant System), which is considerably more established than the sector median dividend growth streak of 3 years. This explains the A grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from last January).

ABT's dividend is highly sustainable as well, which sets it up to build on this dividend growth streak for the foreseeable future. The company's adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the mid-40% range for 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, that would be comfortably better than the 60% payout ratio that rating agencies desire from the industry. In terms of free cash flow, ABT's dividend is about as well-covered. In 2026, the FCF payout ratio is anticipated to be in the high-40% range.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the risk of a payout reduction over the next 12 months at 0.3%. In the next average recession, this rises to just 1.8%. Unsurprisingly, the Quant System awards a B+ grade for overall dividend safety (up from a B- grade from last January).

Looking ahead, the Quant System forecasts a 6.2% annual forward dividend per share growth rate for ABT (the same as it was last January). That would be better than the sector median of 4.8%. And given the company's reputation for high single-digit annual dividend growth, this is arguably a bit conservative. I could foresee dividend growth being closer to its 3-year CAGR of 7.7% in the years ahead. At any rate, ABT earns an A grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (unchanged from last January).

Risks to Consider

ABT arguably has a bright future ahead. Still, some risks could materially impact the investment thesis.

The first notable risk that ABT faces is its pending acquisition of Exact Sciences. This deal could potentially face antitrust challenges. If that does happen, significant divestitures could be required to get the deal across the finish line. What's more, there is execution risk if this acquisition is ultimately finalized. If ABT can't scale Exact's direct-to-consumer model and navigate the changing reimbursement landscape for multi-cancer early detection tests, its success could be diminished.

Another risk to the company is that the majority of its net sales in 2025 (61.4%) were derived outside the United States (ABT's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release). Unfavorable foreign currency translation could pressure its financial results in the future. More importantly, ABT must comply with a wide range of regulations from one market to the next. If it fails to do so, it could be hit by regulators with sizable penalties.

One final risk facing ABT is the overhang of multidistrict litigation (MDL) regarding its cow's-milk-based preterm infant formulas. As of January 2026, there were around 950 total cases filed, with 769 pending in the MDL. The plaintiffs allege that ABT failed to warn them that its cow's milk products could raise the risk of low birth weight infants (preemies) developing necrotizing enterocolitis by anywhere from six to 20 times versus preemies who were fed breast milk.

Thus far, ABT has won the first three bellwether federal cases via summary judgment for insufficient evidence that cow's milk formula was the specific cause of NEC in impacted infants. Still, the company has more cases against which it must defend itself. The first is likely to get underway this August.

In state-level courts that are sometimes viewed as more plaintiff-friendly, ABT may not fare as well. If it can't get such verdicts overturned, this could potentially cost it billions of dollars in legal damages.

Summary: The Market Selloff is a Gift

It's not every day that a Dividend King sells off 9% following earnings. In my view, this represents a clear buying opportunity. ABT has the catalysts to deliver double-digit percentage adjusted diluted EPS growth. The balance sheet is healthy, too.

And while ABT does face integration risk with its Exact Sciences acquisition and pending litigation, that's arguably priced into the valuation. This provides a realistic path to 12%+ annual total returns over the next five years. That's why I'm standing by my Buy rating.

data.title: When Quality Goes On Sale: 2 Dividend Growth Stocks To Consider For Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

One of my biggest lessons in investing, I would say, is my lack of patience. While I've gotten better, this is still something I often struggle with today.

I say that to say that if you're patient enough, the market will always present some compelling investment opportunities for those who are patient. And these contrarian opportunities can lead to potential outperformance.

In reference to these two stocks I discuss today, both have been beaten down in the past year. Whether it's from tariffs, higher-for-longer interest rates impacting consumers, or even overvaluation.

As a result, both currently trade well below their five-year averages, a potentially compelling buying investment opportunity for long-term investors. And this is despite lingering risks from inflation, economic uncertainty, and changing consumer behavior.

In this article, I discuss why these two stocks are underperforming, their fundamentals, and why long-term dividend growth investors should consider them for their portfolio.

T-Mobile (TMUS)

T-Mobile is a stock that has underperformed in the past year. As you can see in the chart below, the stock has significantly underperformed the market in the past year, down close to 16%. The S&P 500 (SP500) is up close to 17% in comparison.

In my opinion, I believe this has less to do with T-Mobile's fundamentals and more to do with them being overvalued. I discussed this in an article back in July. As a result, I downgraded the stock from a buy to a hold.

At the time, TMUS was trading around $240 a share and up 39% at the time of writing. Back then, I warned investors that the biggest risk was the valuation and that they should consider adding closer to the $200 level.

Since then, the price has continued to slide, leading me to believe the telecom giant is now in oversold territory. TMUS recently reached a new 52-week low of $184 and currently sits slightly above, around $185 a share.

At the time of writing, T-Mobile's attractive forward P/E of 18x appears to be an attractive entry point using the earnings estimate.

For long-term investors, this should also be viewed as an attractive entry as the current price sits well below its five-year average. Below, analysts are expecting TMUS to see strong growth of 18.66% through 2030.

For a stock expected to see near 20% growth, I think a P/E of 22x - 25x is very reasonable. Besides their upside potential, one of the most attractive metrics about TMUS is the dividend growth since initiating one in late 2023.

While telecom companies are known for solid payout ratios around 60%, TMUS's free cash flow payout ratio in their last quarter was 21.6%. This is compared to 54.4% for peer Verizon (VZ).

TMUS raised their dividend back in September by an impressive 16%. And since initiating one in 2023, the dividend has grown by an impressive 57%, from $0.65 to $1.02.

Despite their short dividend growth track record, the low payout ratio leads me to believe that TMUS will continue to grow the dividend by double digits for at least the next five years.

Moreover, frequent share repurchases will likely enable solid earnings growth, leading to additional dividend increases and upside potential. Using the fair value of 24.42x, TMUS offers strong upside to the price target of roughly $393 a share by the end of 2028.

Investors may view TMUS' debt balance as a problem due to high-interest rates. But the low payout ratio gives plenty of retained cash flow to pay down debt.

Moreover, their leverage ratio of 2.4x is reasonable. For comparison, Verizon had the lowest of the peer group at 2.2x. AT&T (T) had the highest at 2.59x.

Although risks linger from increased competition that could result in a potential loss of subscribers, any further volatility should be viewed as a buying opportunity.

However, I believe the company's frequent buybacks, acquisitions, and lower base rates will provide tailwinds, allowing TMUS to continue to dominate competitors. And I suspect the telecom giant could return to its normal valuation in the next 12 to 24 months.

#2 Otis Worldwide (OTIS)

Otis is another out-of-favor stock at the moment. Like T-Mobile, the elevator giant is also in the red in the past year, down more than 6%. Since declining after Q2 earnings from above $100 a share, the stock seems to have bottomed out around $85 and has since traded sideways.

Otis' problems, in my opinion, have stemmed from the higher-for-longer interest rate environment and tariffs, which have impacted margins.

Since August, OTIS has been trading in a range of $85 to $90 a share, a buying opportunity for long-term investors. Due to margin pressures, the company suffered a 20% decline in its New Equipment segment, led by headwinds in China.

During Q3 earnings, operating margins declined from 6.4% to 4.7% year-over-year. Additionally, the sell-off this past summer was led by a revision in guidance by management in the second quarter.

In the latest quarter, pressures continued to weigh on the company. But cost savings initiatives of $240 million by fiscal year's end are expected to mitigate headwinds. They also raised their EPS outlook in Q3, representing 5%–7% growth for the fiscal year.

Additionally, Otis controls a large part of the market share in the elevator industry at close to 37% at the end of 2023. And despite headwinds likely to continue for at least the near term, Otis remains a compelling investment for patient investors willing to hold for the next three to five years.

Lower base rates should provide tailwinds. Frequent share repurchases, retained cash flow, and a solid balance sheet put the company in a favorable position to successfully navigate current headwinds.

Otis only has $7.6 billion in long-term debt and $840 million in cash available. The free cash flow payout ratio through three quarters was solid at 71.8%. For the fiscal year, free cash flow is expected to be $1.45 billion, a slight increase from $1.437 in 2024.

As a reference point, Otis ended 2024 with a free cash flow payout ratio of 37.5%. This gives them ample room for future dividend increases and buybacks. In Q3, management exhausted their $800 million buyback program.

And long term, this will likely continue to drive earnings growth in the high, single-digit range, in my opinion. For the next four years, earnings are expected to grow at a CAGR of 11.6%.

As a result, I believe this should allow solid dividend growth in the 5%–8% range as well. Like T-Mobile, Otis doesn't have a long dividend track record, initiating a dividend in 2020.

Since then, the dividend has more than doubled from $0.20 to $0.42 a share. Their last increase was 7.7% to $0.42. And as previously mentioned, I foresee mid- to high single-digit increases going forward, making OTIS a solid wealth compounder for long-term investors.

Like TMUS, the elevator giant also offers strong upside with a price target of $141 by the end of 2028. Furthermore, the stock received an upgrade in December due to diminishing China headwinds and trades below its five-year average of 24.66x.

One of Otis' risks still includes tariffs. Recently, President Trump threatened to impose tariffs on several European nations starting next month. And these are expected to increase in June if no deal is reached.

Currently, Otis expects organic sales growth in the mid-single digits. However, if tariffs are indeed imposed, this may impact this, leading to further volatility. But investors should view this as a buying opportunity to dollar-cost average.

Investor Takeaway

T-Mobile and Otis are great long-term dividend growers with strong fundamentals and solid moats.

Long-term investors should view their price underperformances as buying opportunities, as I believe lower base rates should provide tailwinds going forward.

As a result, I expect both could return to their fair value ratios in the next 12 to 24 months.

Despite lingering risks from macro factors that could continue to impact margins, I'm confident both companies can return to double-digit growth in the near to medium future

data.title: Northeast Bank's Surge Doesn't Necessitate A Downgrade
data.description:
I have, over the last few years now, established a pretty solid track record when it comes to banks. One company that I have recently gotten very right is Northeast Bank (NBN). With a market capitalization of $921.3 million as of this writing, it is certainly not the largest bank that I have analyzed. But it is far from the smallest as well. Over the last several months now, I have been bullish about the institution. And that bullishness has paid off nicely. Since I reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate back in October of last year, the stock has risen 15.3%. That dwarfs the 3.6% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I upgraded the stock to a ‘buy’ in June of last year, shares have achieved an upside of 31.4%. That's very close to double the 16.9% increase that the market enjoyed.

Looking at the picture today, I see a bank that continues to achieve some nice growth. The balance sheet is improving, and that's taking the income statement up with it. Asset quality remains high, though the stock is not as cheap as it once was. The good news is that, even with the share price increase that we have seen, shares are cheap enough to justify my previous bullish assessment. That's why I don't have an issue reaffirming it as a soft ‘buy’ candidate at this time.

It is always possible that my mind could change on the matter as new data comes in. We do know that, around the end of the month, tentatively January 26th, the management team at Northeast Bank will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts seem mixed, with revenue forecasted to rise while profitability drops. But whatever transpires, the devil will be in the details.

Too Early to Downgrade Northeast Bank

For the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, which is the only new quarter for which we have data available, Northeast Bank posted mixed results. For instance, the value of deposits did unfortunately drop, declining from $3.38 billion down to $3.25 billion. But the good news is that all of this decline was driven by brokered deposits, which are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial firms. So even though this decline in the size of the balance sheet is disappointing in and of itself, where that decline came from was as good as it could have been.

As deposits for the institution decline, we did see a small drop in the value of loans. But honestly, it amounts to a rounding error in the grand scheme of things. And I do mean that quite literally. From the end of 2025 to the first quarter of 2026, the value of loans dipped by only about $2 million. That was enough to keep the overall amount at about $3.71 billion. The vast majority of the loan portfolio is tied up in commercial real estate. Management had about $2.79 billion dedicated to that category in the first quarter. A distant second place belongs to commercial and industrial, with $849.6 million. The rest of the portfolio, meanwhile, is comprised of residential real estate and just a modicum of consumer loans.

Other aspects of the balance sheet should not be ignored. It is important to note, however, that the other parts of the balance sheet are quite small by comparison. For instance, the company had only $15.6 million of securities on its books in the first quarter of 2026. That's down from the $22.7 million reported at the end of 2025. Cash and cash equivalents went from $413.6 million down to $341.8 million. And at the same time as this, debt dropped slightly from $320.2 million to $316.8 million.

Normally when you see multiple areas of the balance sheet shrink, you would expect to see revenue and profits worsen. But surprisingly, that's not what transpired. Management actually saw net interest income grow from $38.6 million to $48.6 million. This was in spite of the fact that the net interest margin for the institution declined from 4.90% to 4.59%. This might all seem contradictory. But what's important here is that, even though we have been talking about changes in the balance sheet since the end of 2024, and those have shown a decline in size year over year for each fiscal quarter, for the first quarter, we have seen a pretty big increase in the size of the balance sheet. For instance, total interest-earning assets jumped, on an average basis, 31.9% year over year. That absolutely can impact net interest income in a big and positive way.

Non-interest income also grew, rising from $4.1 million to $4.7 million. This was thanks to an increase in the gain on sales of SBA loans from $3.3 million to $4.1 million. Without that, we would have actually seen a drop in non-interest income. As a result of these improvements, net profits amounted to $22.5 million during the first quarter of 2026. By comparison, for the same time the previous year, profitability amounted to $17.1 million.

It is too early to really rely much on projections for 2026. But if we were to rely too much on 2026 results, based on what we have seen already for the year, we would be looking at a firm that is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 10.2. This is just a hair above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. And, in the chart above, you can see that the institution comes in below all but one of the five institutions that I compared it to. So on an absolute basis, that isn't exactly undervalued, while on a relative basis, it's on the cheaper end of the spectrum. In the chart below, I then looked at the picture through the lens of the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In addition to being expensive on an absolute basis, it's also significantly more costly than any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart.

The reason for this high-priced book and price-to-tangible book multiple situation can be chalked up to high asset quality as well. In the first chart below, you can see that Northeast Bank has a return on assets reading of 2.13%. That's more than double the 1% maximum that I like to see. And it puts the company tied with one other player as the top prospect in that chart. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 17.64%, Northeast Bank is massively superior to any of the five banks that I compared it to. And it is also comfortably above the 10% minimum that I target.

Other than recent fluctuations and the size of the balance sheet, the only real complaint that I have of the institution is that its credit quality is not quite as high as I would like. I typically prefer, for instance, for no more than 0.50% of loans and assets to be classified as non-performing. But as of the end of the most recent quarter, these numbers amounted to 0.90% and 0.84%, respectively. Both of those, however, represented improvements over what was seen a year earlier, when the company notched readings of 1.06% and 0.94%, respectively.

At the start of this article, I made the case that my opinion on the business could change when new data is released. That is just around the corner, with January 26th estimated for that release date. As we approach that time, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $54.8 million. This would actually be up slightly from the $54.4 million reported a year earlier. On the other hand, profitability is expected to decline, dropping from $2.74 per share to $2.45 per share. This would mean a drop in net profits from $22.4 million to $20.7 million. Obviously, we will need to read deeper into that to understand what all is going on if analysts are correct, and even if they aren't. I generally don't like declining profits. But given the overall health of the company, that would likely be more of a bump in the road than anything.

Takeaway

On the whole, I would say that Northeast Bank is most certainly an interesting prospect. It's not exactly the best candidate out there. But it's definitely not the worst either. The stock has performed well in the time that I have been bullish about it. And honestly, at some point, it will make sense to downgrade it. But for now, I believe that maintaining it as a soft ‘buy’ is the appropriate choice.

data.title: I'm Betting Big On These 8-10% Yields For Early Retirement
data.description:
High-yield stocks are a great way to invest for early retirement because they enable you to accelerate the growth of your passive income stream to fund your living expenses once you stop working. Additionally, the way in which they deliver their returns, namely through passive income from dividends, is generally much more reliable than depending on market performance with an index investing strategy.

While investing in dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) or the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM) are simple and popular investment vehicles for retiring on dividends, their yields range between 3% and 4% and are often too low for many investors looking to retire early. Therefore, high-yielding stocks can be a more attractive alternative.

However, the big risk with high-yield stocks is that their high yields are unsustainable, and you end up making deep dividend cuts that not only result in disappointing passive income for investors but also lead to steep share price declines, leaving investors in the worst possible shape. Additionally, even if they do sustain their payouts, they often fail to deliver enough passive income to both meet current living expenses and grow that income to keep pace with inflation over time.

As a result, investors need to be highly selective in what high-yield stocks they pick. With this in view, in this article, I’m going to share two high-yield opportunities that I’m betting big on for early retirement.

8% Yielding Opportunity With Strong Growth Potential

The first opportunity I will discuss is MPLX LP (MPLX). MPLX is a midstream MLP that issues a K-1 tax form. So investors should keep the tax implications of this in mind before investing in it. Of course, you can invest in 1099-issuing ETF alternatives like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), which can still give you a similar distribution yield, strong distribution growth, and exposure to MPLX, along with several other midstream businesses, without having to deal with the K-1 tax form, though you will have to pay a substantial expense ratio of 0.85% and have a less advantageous tax treatment than you would enjoy by simply owning individual MLPs like MPLX.

MPLX is a pick that I feel confident betting big on because it combines a high yield with a healthy balance sheet, a strong business model, and attractive long-term growth prospects. Its balance sheet is strong, as evidenced by its BBB credit rating and its well-diversified, well-laddered debt maturities through 2030, with no more than $2 billion maturing each year and, in some years, even less than $1 billion maturing. Meanwhile, its leverage ratio remains well below its upper limit of four times, which gives it significant flexibility to be able to repurchase units, invest in attractive opportunities that come its way, and continue to grow its distribution at an aggressive clip.

Moreover, its business model is durable and defensive, with limited commodity price exposure thanks to having a highly contracted business model that is diversified across crude oil, product logistics, natural gas, and NGL services. Its crude oil and products logistics business is highly integrated with Marathon Petroleum’s (MPC) value chains, which have a competitive advantage and are competitively positioned within their regions.

Additionally, its natural gas and NGL services handle over 10% of all natural gas produced in the United States. MPLX also has strong growth potential, as it is leaning aggressively into its natural gas and NGL opportunities, with it accelerating its capital expenditures from $0.9 billion in 2022 to $1.2 billion in 2023 to $1.9 billion in 2024 to a whopping $5.5 billion in 2025, with the vast majority of that coming from strategic bolt-on acquisitions. Additionally, over 90% of its 2025 growth capital was spent on natural gas and NGLs, as it is leaning into the powerful growth tailwinds behind those sectors.

These all combine to provide strong support for its attractive distribution that currently yields 8% on a next 12-month basis, and management has guided for double-digit distribution growth for the foreseeable future. Analysts also see this as a likely outcome, as they are forecasting an 11.5% distribution CAGR through 2027.

Putting this all together makes MPLX one of the most compelling combinations of high current yield and inflation-beating growth backed by a durable and defensive business model.

10%+ Yielding Opportunity With a Strong Balance Sheet

The second investment that I’m betting big on for retirement is Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC). While many BDCs (BIZD) - such as FS KKR (FSK) - are seeing their dividends cut or appear highly likely to be cut in the near future, KBDC has been able to support its payout and appears positioned to be able to continue doing so. This is because it currently covers its dividend by 1.08 times with its net investment income, while also having a leverage ratio of just 1.01 times. A leverage ratio at that level gives it significant additional debt capacity that it can put onto its balance sheet to enhance its return on equity and further support its base dividend before it reaches its upper limit of 1.25 times.

Meanwhile, KBDC’s portfolio is in a strong position thanks to having 94% of its investments in first-lien senior secured loans, with its investments diversified across 108 borrowers spread across numerous industries.

Additionally, it has no near-term refinancing headwinds, as its closest maturity is in 2027 and is fixed interest rate debt with a rate of 8.65% that I am quite confident they could refinance today at a materially lower interest rate if it were to mature. The only reason they are not able to refinance it early is that prepayment penalties would be applied to them if they paid it off early and would make it uneconomical to do so. Nevertheless, they should only see tailwinds moving forward on the interest expense side of things, since much of their debt is floating rate, and the small portion that is fixed rate is at higher interest rates that they should be able to refinance at lower interest rates in the coming years.

This gives me even further confidence in their ability to support their dividend moving forward. Not only that, but their 1.01 times leverage ratio is further overstated in terms of their actual earnings power because, during the quarter, they had an average debt-to-equity of 0.97 times.

On top of that, the portfolio health remains quite strong, with only 1.4% of the portfolio, on a fair value basis, on non-accrual. The company is also buying back stock through its share purchase program to take advantage of the current discount to net asset value and help to add leverage to the value, further optimizing returns. These buybacks are accretive on an accretion basis and also help to enhance the dividend coverage ratio.

Additionally, KBDC currently yields 10.8% and trades at a 9.6% discount to its net asset value. Therefore, it is undervalued, has a clear path to supporting its very attractive dividend for the foreseeable future, and while I do not expect it to generate anything in the way of dividend growth, its yield is high enough that you could simply retain a portion of its dividends and reinvest them to further grow the dividend payout of the portfolio as a whole while still generating a high single-digit yield to support living expenses.

Investor Takeaway

While I like these investment opportunities quite a bit right now, neither of these opportunities is risk-free. For example, MPLX’s aggressive growth investments could encounter hiccups through project delays or running over budget, and the unit price will be influenced in the short term by commodity price volatility. However, I view these risks as fairly low, as MPLX has an excellent track record of generating attractive returns on its projects, and the long-term value of MPLX is closely tied to the cash flow it generates from its long-duration contracts and its ability to support and grow its distribution at a solid clip over time.

Meanwhile, KBDC is a relatively unproven BDC, which does raise some concerns about its underwriting skill during an economic downturn. However, its conservative structure, strong underwriting performance, attractive valuation, and adequate coverage of its very attractive dividend yield give me confidence in its risk-reward profile as a high-yield income machine that could support living expenses in an early retirement scenario.

That being said, these securities are far from buy-and-throw-away investments, as you do need to actively monitor and manage them so that, at the first sign of trouble, you can slowly allocate the capital into a more durable income instrument at that time. However, at the moment, neither of them shows signs of issues. As a result, they occupy meaningful positions in my 7-8% yielding, low-beta, and attractive-returning retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Lamb Weston Holdings: Upgrade To Buy On Earnings Growth Potential
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Lamb Weston Holdings (LW) in late 2024, as I saw no visible upside in the near term that could turn the sentiment around. Things have changed after a year or so; volumes in North America are growing again, utilization is improving, and management has shown a willingness to sacrifice price to rebuild demand. Capital allocation (dividends and share buyback) also sets a new floor to the stock.

North American Inflection

Back in 2024, my view was that the North American business looked fundamentally broken. Volumes were declining, consumer traffic was under pressure, and LW was really relying on pricing to grow (an unsustainable move). Without volume growth, I believed the equity was essentially uninvestable. A year has passed, and I am finally seeing the right data points to prove me wrong. There is a clear inflection in North American volumes since my last update. Volume in this region has been up for 4 straight quarters, with the latest quarter volume up 8%, a stark change vs. the sharp decline I saw previously. And what makes this very bullish is that this recovery does not appear to be purely macro-driven. Look around the US economy; it is not as if consumers are suddenly splurging again. What the data suggests is that LW is winning share, and we can infer this by comparing LW’s North American volume growth to the muted broader US restaurant sales growth.

This traction of market share gains should continue as LW has recalibrated its commercial strategy. Basically, they did what I expected. Management cut prices to win volumes, and the numbers say it all. North America volume grew 8%, but price/mix fell 7%. The market clearly doesn’t like this move (as you can see from the share price reaction), but I believe it was an inevitable move. As I said, LW cannot rely on price increases forever; at some point it has to bite the bullet and “reset” the pricing level. That clearly worked. LW managed to secure new customer contract wins and drove growth across all channels. This strengthens the growth profile. LW now has a larger base of recurring demand, which should drive up utilization of its capacity, resulting in higher incremental margins.

We can literally see these incremental margins showing up in the P&L. Despite the sharp drop in price/mix, EBIT grew 15% y/y to $146 million. Take a moment and let that sink in. LW’s net sales were only up 1% y/y, with pricing down, and yet EBIT was up double digits. That is the strongest proof point that utilization matters more than price in this business. I think the idea is very easy to grasp. LW has multiple factories that are all fixed cost. Whether volume is high or low, the lights are on and workers are in it. When volume is below a certain threshold, margins get wiped out, but the same is true otherwise. If this is the pace of volume growth that we are seeing, which drives a much higher EBIT growth, I am all for management to aggressively reset prices to win more volume.

Capital Allocation is a Bullish Signal

In my opinion, another bullish development is LW’s latest capital allocation decision. The Board has approved a 3% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.38 per share and has also repurchased ~$40 million of common stock (retiring >600,000 shares in Q2 2026). If these figures are the run rate, we are looking at a 5+% yield from capital allocation. This is huge because it sets another “floor” to the stock valuation. Previously, that floor was if a strategic buyer like Post Holdings (POST) eventually stepped in, which is really something outside of LW’s control. But today, that “floor” is controlled by LW, and I like the signal management is sending to investors, that at these levels, the shares are attractive.

Valuation

I see a path for LW’s share price to more than double over the next two years, and the assumptions are not aggressive. LW is already growing revenue by 1%, which I expect to accelerate to mid-single-digits as the pricing strategy works out, and with that, margins should easily expand back to >10% (FY23 level as a benchmark) because of operating leverage. As such, while revenue growth is in the low-to-mid single digits, earnings growth is substantially higher. If LW is printing this magnitude of earnings growth, which it was expected to do back in late 2022, it deserves to trade at that previous valuation level, which is >20x forward PE. I am assuming the multiple goes back to LW’s historical average (21x), which translates to a price target of $102.

Investment Risk

While the North American turnaround is very encouraging, the international segment is still a drag. Depending on the magnitude of the decline moving forward, the North American improvements may be overwhelmed. In addition, the bull case relies heavily on operating leverage kicking in. But if the decline in price/mix is so much that it offsets the incremental margins from higher volumes, earnings growth may be a lot weaker than I thought.

Conclusion

I upgrade my recommendation to a buy rating for LW. North America is no longer a drag; pricing has been reset to drive utilization, and operating leverage is clearly flowing through the P&L. On top of that, dividends and buybacks now set a floor to the stock valuation. If LW can grow revenue and expand margin as I assumed, valuation should go up, and the upside could be more than double.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Winter Storm Brewing, Carts Filling
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Winter storm watch: Costco (COST) is set to benefit from the pantry-loading phenomenon. (00:14) Capital One (COF) to buy fintech Brex for $5.15B. (02:18) Earnings Snapshot: Intel (INTC) tops Q4 estimates but initiates Q1 outlook below forecast. (03:01)

This is an abridged transcript.

A winter storm is set to rock the South, Midwest, and Northeast parts of the U.S. from today through Monday. According to The Weather Channel, the impact of the storm could reach half the U.S. population.

The forecast calls for snow, sleet, and freezing rain to develop today from the southern Rockies and Plains into Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and parts of the Mid-South, then spread east into the Tennessee Valley, Appalachians, and Carolinas through Saturday night. By Sunday, the storm will shift into the Ohio Valley, Mid-Atlantic, and Northeast, with increasing odds for heavy snow along the I-95 corridor from Washington, D.C. to Boston and lingering impacts into early Monday.

Mizuho Securities analyst David Bellinger said the firm expects Costco (COST) to benefit in the near term from the well-publicized storm. He noted that Costco (COST) has typically seen a surge in sales and traffic as large-scale events approach and as consumers pantry load in preparation. For investors, the pantry-loading effect for Costco (COST) is considered likely to help the U.S. business put up a strong sales growth tally compared to a year ago when a polar vortex also drove traffic.

Anecdotal posts on social media show Costco (COST) stores crowded in states such as Texas, Georgia, Tennessee, and Florida.

Shares of Costco (COST) are up 13% year-to-date.

On the flip side, dine-in restaurants, malls, and discretionary brick-and-mortar retail are likely to see a Q1 revenue headwind from the multi-day period of consumers sheltering inside.

Restaurant companies such as Dunkin', Darden Restaurants (DRI), Brinker International (EAT), BJ's Restaurants (BJ), Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), Shake Shack (SHAK), Sweetgreen (SG), and McDonald's (MCD) have all cited severe winter weather in the past as a drag on traffic.

Meanwhile, CenterPoint Energy (CNP) said earlier this week that it has added more than 600 frontline workers, bringing its total workforce to 3,300, as the company prepares for the storm.

Capital One Financial (COF) has agreed to acquire Brex for $5.15B.

Brex is a fintech company that specializes in technology for credit cards, expenses, and rewards.

The purchase price consists of 50% cash and 50% stock. With the acquisition, Capital One (COF), which acquired Discover Financial last year, gets more capabilities to cater to corporate clients. The AI-native software platform was created to make it easier for businesses to issue corporate cards, automate expense management, and make secure, real-time payments, the companies said.

Brex was founded in 2017. Capital One (COF) expects to close the deal in mid-2026, the company said.

Now an update to our story from Thursday’s edition of Wall Street Breakfast.

Intel (INTC) reported earnings after the bell.

Shares are down 12% in premarket action after reporting stronger-than-expected fourth-quarter results, but issuing guidance for the coming quarter that was below Wall Street's forecast.

Intel expects first-quarter revenue to be between $11.7B and $12.7B, with the midpoint of $12.2B below the $12.56B analysts were anticipating. Intel expects adjusted earnings per share to break even, below the $0.08 analysts expected. It also anticipates adjusted gross margins of 32.3%.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Amazon's another round of layoffs starts next week - report

Downtown Las Vegas casino owner gets creative in trying to convince Canadians to return

Netflix says Paramount bid for Warner Bros ‘doesn’t pass the sniff test’ - FT

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 1% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.5% at $89,000. Gold is down 0.3% at $4,917.

The FTSE 100 is little changed and the DAX is up 0.2%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Ericsson (ERIC) +8% - Shares jumped after delivering stronger-than-expected Q4 results.

Economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment

  • 10:00 am Leading Indicators

And don’t forget to add the Investing Experts Live: Top Ideas for 2026 event to your calendar for next week. The live event is scheduled for Tuesday, January 27th at noon. One of the discussions will cover growth and tech investing strategy for this year. Here’s a link to register for this free event.

data.title: Investor Emotional Cycle Is Alive And Well
data.description:
Madness and mayhem are the order of the day, while consumer and business sentiment are dour, and for-sale signs are popping up like measles.

Meanwhile, equity markets are priced for nirvana, and participants are the most long of all time. Households and nonprofit organizations entered 2026 with a record near-half of their financial assets held in equities - the most ever (shown below since 1952).

Professional fund managers are positioned with the highest equity exposure and growth expectations since the market top in 2021 (shown below since 2001, courtesy of The Daily Shot).

At the same time, global managers are holding the least downside protection hedges since the 2021 market top (below since 2008).

Fund manager cash levels at 3.2% are the lowest on record since at least 1999 (shown below). The bear market is in dry powder; cash on hand has never been so low.

As real estate investors have been discovering, when sellers wish/need to sell en masse at high prices, there are few willing and able buyers to be found. Spring listings are looking to start early this year. See, BMO economist says real estate has entered a ‘slow grind’ toward affordability:

Andre Kutyan, broker with Harvey Kalles Real Estate, began photographing the pools and landscaping of some houses last fall in preparation for hitting the market early in the New Year, when they may still be buried under a layer of snow.

He plans to start launching new listings in mid-January.

“I’m curious to see how early the spring market will start this year,” he says.

Agents are also watching to see how much supply arrives on the resale market.

Many are hearing from baby boomers who have been holding onto their large homes with hopes of a recovery in prices, but have now decided to move ahead with a sale.

Some homeowners with high-priced properties are also choosing to downsize their investment as economic uncertainty continues.

“You can only wait so long,” he [BMO economist, Robert Kavcic] says of the sellers. “There’s a cost to waiting, too.”

The attitude among buyers at the moment appears to be a reverse FOMO, or “fear of missing out,” he says.

This is all classic. Humans are wired to self-sabotage in financial matters, and it takes discipline and forethought to resist groupthink. Opportunity arrives for patient buyers holding cash who’ve prepared their buy targets in advance.

The investor emotional cycle has not been repealed, and we now have emojis to help express it.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The South Park Market Of 2026
data.description:
I have been a “South Park” fan for as long as I can remember, and while the show isn’t a market guidebook, its brutal satire cuts through nonsense better than many Wall Street commentaries. Just like on the show, characters make absurd decisions and face absurd consequences, which is familiar to investors today. For example, one of my favorite scenes is when Stanley goes to the bank to deposit the money his Grandmother gave him into an account at the bank… “And It’s Gone.”

Notably, in South Park, Eric Cartman once declared, “Screw you guys, I’m going home.” That line has become shorthand for frustration and fatigue when chaos overwhelms you during market volatility. For example, during the “Liberation Day” market decline, many investors sold out just as the market reached its bottom. The increase in market volatility was something we wrote about this time last year in “Curb Your Enthusiasm.” Notably, the same market dynamics that existed then persist today. Such suggests that investing in 2026 may also experience similar increases in volatility. That means anyone looking for a simple road map will end up feeling like Cartman walking out on his friends.

To avoid being Cartman, it is critical to understand that 2026 will not deliver certainty. Instead, investors should focus and make decisions based on probabilities backed by data, earnings trends, policy shifts, and macro signals. Wall Street analysts have already begun issuing universally bullish forecasts, some more cautious than others. However, while there are no guarantees of outcomes, a shifting environment of volatility and complexity is expected.

This article breaks the 2026 outlook into three cornerstone sections:

  1. Market Structure and Valuations
  2. Economic Forces and Policy Drivers
  3. Strategic Investment Implications

Across each section, you will see the same economic truth South Park illustrates: Chaos is not the opposite of order. Chaos is part of the system.

Market Structure and Valuations in 2026

Wall Street begins 2026 with conflicting signals. As noted above, while several Wall Street firms expect to see another year of “double-digit” gains in 2026, such follows three consecutive years of elevated gains. That is potentially a risk, as discussed in the “Market Outlook For 2026.”

“The current 3-year return is 18% above its 3-year average. While that is not the highest level on record, when the index trades significantly above its moving average, volatility tends to rise. These periods often see sharp drawdowns, and corrections become more frequent, with increased variance in returns leading to larger losses in downturns, which compounds the problem. Secondly, there are declining risk-adjusted returns. When returns deviate significantly from the trend, future returns tend to revert toward the mean. This mean reversion is driven by stretched valuations resetting. Over time, high volatility and large price swings reduce compound returns. Even if average returns remain positive, the math of compounding is compromised by losses, weakening full-cycle gains.”

Secondly, the markets currently trade at record levels above their long-term exponential growth trend. As shown in the chart below, when valuations rise unchecked, the market grossly exceeds its long-term exponential growth trend, eventually leading to a reversion.

Lastly, valuations remain above long‑term historical norms. Crucially, as discussed in a recent #BullBearReport:

“Market valuation measures are just that—a measure of current valuation. Moreover, market valuations are a much better measure of “investor psychology” and a manifestation of the “greater fool theory.” This is why a high correlation exists between one-year trailing valuations and consumer confidence in higher stock prices.”

Simply, this means that current valuations are just a reflection of the “hope” that future earnings growth and profitability will justify overpaying for assets today. Wall Street’s 2026 forecast for the markets represents that “hope.”

However, what we do know today is that the most powerful force in finance is “mean reversion.” As all the charts above demonstrate, at some point over the next five years, market returns will likely be closer to 0% than 10%.

Of course, that doesn’t mean markets will “crash” in 2026. However, price deviations, excess valuations, and elevated sentiment do suggest that market volatility will likely be higher and returns lower than current expectations. Such is particularly the case if something happens that causes Wall Street to lower forward earnings expectations. It is worth noting that historically, valuations have not always caught up with earnings, but rather vice versa.

That scenario is when the Cartman line feels most appropriate: “Screw you guys, I’m going home.” Because investors will shift from an optimistic to a defensive position almost instantly.

Drivers Shaping 2026

Markets do not exist in a vacuum. Earnings “expectations” are what will matter the most, but earnings are influenced by macro forces such as inflation, interest rates, global growth, and monetary policy. Currently, many of those factors are not favorable to those more elevated forecasts.

For example, inflation remains a central concern, with major investment banks and strategists cautioning that inflation may hover above the Federal Reserve’s 2% target as the labor market weakens. Jerome Powell even noted this in the December FOMC policy statement. During Powell’s press conference, he emphasized that job gains have slowed and downside risks to employment have increased. That statement aligns with our recent article on how alternative employment sources may affect its outlook. Powell went further, suggesting official payroll figures likely overstate job growth by around 60,000 jobs per month. That implies an actual labor market contraction. It further acknowledges that the potential negative payroll growth was a pivotal signal of the Fed’s priorities.

Such is crucial to forward expectations because the markets are closely tied to both fiscal and monetary actions. While the Fed signaled possible rate cuts in 2026, if inflation does not subside as expected, rate cuts may be limited or postponed, which would negatively impact investor confidence. Famed investors often warn that policy surprises matter more than policy intentions, especially when markets are priced for optimism.

Secondly, internationally, growth forecasts are uneven. Credit rating agencies project moderate but uneven expansion across major economies, with emerging markets often outpacing developed markets in GDP growth. That imbalance creates divergence in asset returns and credit flows. However, given that the Euro area is expected to grow at roughly half the rate of the US, it would be unsurprising to see 2025’s outperformance by international markets, relative to the US, reverse.

In such environments, currency fluctuations and trade dynamics become even more crucial, meaning that a strong U.S. dollar rally or slowing global trade could pressure multinational earnings even if domestic sales hold up.

Lastly, leverage is a growing risk of market volatility disruption. As we discussed in “The DPI Link To Margin Debt,” household allocations to equities are at a record. Of course, such should be unsurprising given the strong market advances over the past few years.

However, this surge in allocations has also been accompanied by a massive expansion in leverage. Currently, margin debt as a percentage of real DPI has been reported at around 6.23%, the highest on record. This ratio also suggests that for every $100 of real DPI, roughly $6 of margin debt is outstanding, a substantial amount. But that number doesn’t include the additional leverage taken on by investors through speculative option trading and 2x and 3x leveraged ETFs, which are also being bought on margin.

As we concluded:

“Naturally, when fresh savings are lacking and investors turn to margin to participate in markets, two risks emerge.“

  1. The quality of the investor base weakens because borrowed money replaces savings.
  2. The carrying cost of that borrowing becomes more salient when interest rates are elevated. If the margin debt carries higher interest and investors’ income growth is weak, servicing the debt becomes harder, reducing the buffer against loss.

“In summary, weak DPI growth, combined with elevated margin borrowing, creates a vulnerability. In such an environment, the investor base is much less resilient.”

Does any of this mean the markets are going to crash in 2026? No.

However, investors should be aware that all these forces interact in complex ways. Recognizing that macroeconomic data releases, policy shifts, and geopolitical events can lead to short-term volatility and long-term trend adjustments is crucial for navigating the “South Park” market in 2026.

South Park Wisdom for Investors

South Park often shows characters reacting emotionally to chaos. Yet mature investors must behave differently and recognize that emotion is not a strategy. However, understanding the data, identifying the probabilities versus possibilities, and maintaining risk controls are critical to success in 2026.

If you feel like shouting, “Screw you guys, I’m going home,” in the face of market moves, take a beat. Markets do not need your commitment; they reward discipline and patience.

In 2026, implement portfolio tactics to participate with the market if the bullish forecasts come to fruition, but protect your wealth in case they don’t.

  • Diversify beyond tech leaders: Shift allocations gradually toward underappreciated sectors, such as healthcare, industrials, energy, and consumer staples, which demonstrate earnings strength and stable demand.
  • Rebalance quarterly: Trim overextended positions, especially in momentum names. Reallocate into lagging but fundamentally sound assets.
  • Build cash buffers: Hold 5% to 15% in short‑duration cash equivalents to deploy during volatility or price dislocations.
  • Use tactical hedges: Add protective puts or inverse ETFs to limit downside on core equity holdings in concentrated portfolios.
  • Add exposure to high‑quality bonds: Reallocate from speculative credit into high‑grade corporate or Treasury ladders as yields remain favorable.
  • Favor earnings momentum over hype: Focus on stocks delivering real EPS growth over those driven by thematic speculation or valuation expansion alone.
  • Reduce concentration in crowded trades: Limit exposure to AI and mega‑cap tech where positioning is crowded and narrative risk is high.
  • Be cautious with international diversification: Developed market and emerging market ETFs are at risk of slower future growth and may be overvalued relative to their respective economies.
  • Adjust risk models for higher volatility: Updating drawdown expectations and rebalancing portfolio risk targets accordingly.
  • Track earnings revisions monthly: Stay nimble and reduce positions in companies issuing weaker forward guidance or showing margin compression.
  • Treat narratives as signals, not strategies: Use themes like AI, reshoring, or inflation narratives to inform ideas, but confirm with real data and balance sheet strength.

These actions position your portfolio for resilience, rather than reliance on a singlescenario, while preserving flexibility to capitalize on market shifts throughout 2026. Like any complex system, markets blend risk and opportunity. Recognizing where probability exceeds speculation is the difference between reacting like Cartman and investing with purpose.

Stay informed, stay disciplined, and treat “chaos” as part of the landscape, not a reason to abandon the field.

That is how you navigate the market in 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bloom Energy: A Boon For Data Center Energy Sovereignty
data.description:
Investment thesis

AI's supercycle continues, bringing with it a need for data centers, the computing power of which requires enormous amounts of electricity. The lack of energy sovereignty is one of the key barriers to the rapid development of data center infrastructure, increasing the demand for local mini power plants. Bloom Energy (BE) is one of the most promising players in this industry, offering not just a "green alternative" but a full replacement for traditional utilities, since its key advantage is the continuity of power supply to data centers regardless of their location or distance from power grids. I will discuss how the time factor in connecting to the US grid affects the prospects for scaling Bloom Energy's power plants. AI companies such as Google, Microsoft, and Amazon value energy independence and are willing to pay a premium for it. I believe that the issue of energy sovereignty will remain relevant in the future, because the shortage of capacity in the US power grid is the main barrier to scaling data centers.

Previous thesis

Since my last publication on BE, a "Buy" recommendation has already brought investors a 60% return. Our main thesis is based on factors that are expected to drive share price growth, which include the expansion of operations, the record growth in revenue and profits, improvements in forecasts for 2025 and 2026, and expectations of tremendous results from the partnership with Brookfield. For a better understanding of the company's strategy, please refer to the previous article, in particular the section on Bloom's strategic growth catalysts and financial profile. This new article provides a more in-depth analysis of BE's competitive advantage.

Problem of energy sovereignty for US data centers

Bloom Energy is an energy company that focuses on the production and installation of distributed power generation. The company's activities are aimed at creating "mini power plants" that are installed at the point of consumption, avoiding the costs of transmitting electricity through networks. Behind the boom surrounding BE is the fact that the company's main customers increasingly include data center operators, requiring enormous amounts of energy for cloud computing and artificial intelligence applications. Traditionally, the power grid is not designed to handle such high-energy demand, and therefore cannot maintain high connection speeds. By building mini power plants directly at the point of consumption, it allows Bloom Energy to quickly deploy the necessary power generation capacity close to data centers.

Whereas BE was previously viewed as an eco-friendly green energy startup, it is now becoming a full-fledged energy security provider. One of the main challenges facing data centers today is the lengthy process of connecting to the public power grid. Technologically, utilities are unable to quickly upgrade the existing grid. Due to this, in some US states, it often takes 4-7 years to connect new data centers. The purchase of BE is therefore primarily a bet on energy arbitrage, based on the company's ability to quickly deploy mini-stations and offer power connections, something for which customers are willing to pay a premium. Grid power networks wear out and require constant upgrading in addition to expansion. Renewable energy sources, usually solar and wind, need not only extra capacity but also expensive storage batteries. As a result, conventional power grids and alternative energy sources require significant time to lay cables, develop capacity, and install batteries. With Bloom, you can solve this problem in a few months to a year.

Moreover, microgrid technologies offer a number of technological advantages that improve the economic efficiency of data center power supply. For one thing, the traditional power grid still suffers from losses of 5% to 8% of electricity during transmission over shared networks. Bloom's technology allows for the combination of energy sources, including solar panels, batteries, and natural gas. In addition, Bloom's mini-stations are less harmful to the environment, since they do not produce loud noise or emissions and have higher fuel combustion efficiency.

Analyzing the issue of energy sovereignty for data centers in the US, the situation appears to be quite critical. The portfolio of projects to connect new capacity to the grid by the end of 2025 is about 2.8 TW, exceeding the total installed generation capacity at present. Previously, it took about three years to connect to the grid for commercial operation, while now it takes 5-7 years. Due to this, however, only 1 in 5 projects actually reach the connection stage, because many applications are rejected due to increasing costs, which include those arising from grid modernization. That's because this part of the capital investment is shifted onto the shoulders of the customers themselves.

Analyzing the regional aspects of BE's potential development, we should pay attention to those US states where this problem is most critical. This is where Bloom is able to dictate its terms. PJM (Virginia/Ohio) is the most problematic region. It has the longest waiting list for connection to the grid, since a whole row of data centers has been built in this region. The CAISO (California) and ERCOT (Texas) regions also have problems. The cost of connecting to the grid is very high in California, where there is also a shortage of capacity during peak hours. There is a rapid increase in new applications in Texas, leading to instability in terms of timing. Moreover, Texas is more isolated from the rest of the US, so local power generation is more valuable.

Economic and technological effects of Bloom for data centers

Having examined the full depth of the problem of energy sovereignty for US data centers, it is now necessary to answer the following question: why Bloom? In analyzing the economic efficiency of BE for data centers, first of all, the opportunity cost should be calculated, with the time factor equal to the loss. As an example, we consider a data center with a capacity of 50 MW that is planning to launch. Because of a problem connecting to the public grid, however, there is a two-year delay in bringing the facility online. The result is that over two years, data center operators could lose about $100 million in potential revenue. And that's not even counting the ongoing costs that still have to be covered by the operators. A BE setup can get up and running faster (within a few months, up to a year for larger facilities).

Furthermore, compare the cost of electricity in PJM depending on whether you choose the traditional grid or Bloom's service. Let's take an approximate price of natural gas at around $3.50 per MMBtu and calculations for 1 kW per hour. Overall, BE will cost an average of 11.5 cents, whereas using the traditional grid will cost 13 cents. Economic efficiency from savings is 11.5%. It is also worth considering tax efficiency, because in Virginia, data center operators are required to pay Transmission Enhancement Charges. For a data center with a capacity of around 50 MW, this difference of 1.5 cents per kWh translates into savings of around $6 million per year.

Parameter

Traditional Network

Bloom Energy

Basic tariff

~9–11 cents

~7–9 cents

T&D

~2–4 cents

0 cents

Reliability

Requires diesel generators

Built into the system

Final cost

11–15 cents/kWh

10–13 cents/kWh

For several reasons, Bloom Energy is able to provide cheaper electricity compared to the general power grid:

  • The higher efficiency of Bloom servers, at around 60% as opposed to 35-40% for traditional grids, thanks to the use of solid oxide fuel cells.
  • Absence of T&D costs, because Bloom's electricity generation takes place on site, with a direct connection to consumption points.
  • Application of cogeneration, in which the heat generated during electricity production is used for heating or for the reverse process of cooling (through absorption chillers).

Analyzing technological efficiency, we should take into account that the BE service provides a constant load. For one thing, it gives users complete autonomy, because their power supply can operate in "island" mode (with power completely disconnected from the external grid during moments of power transmission instability). In addition, it allows for more efficient use of land resources, since 1 acre of land can generate dozens of times more electricity with Bloom than with solar panels.

Concluding our analysis of effectiveness, consider the following graph, showing the difference of approximately 48 months that allows data center operators to generate early revenue while others wait to connect to public networks from traditional utilities. This graph shows how much faster the cumulative revenue of data centers grows from using Bloom's service, making up for the difference in CAPEX. In this way, a bet on BE is not just about getting a fuel cell; it is about taking advantage of a 4-year head start to generate cumulative revenue when the market is in the midst of an AI supercycle and the fastest technology race in human history.

BE valuation

In assessing BE's potential value, is important to note that its shares are already trading at a growth premium compared to its competitors. But this is because it is the only one that is profitable and has contracts with data centers. Its EV/Sales metric is 14.18x. Nevertheless, the Bloom margin is about 30%, allowing it to transition into the "Growth at a Reasonable Price" category. In my opinion, a fair EV/Sales multiple for such a promising and fast-growing company should be around 18x, given the shortage of data center capacity in the US. This means that BE's fair share price, taking into account the 2026 revenue forecast of $2.51 billion, can be $45.18 billion. It's 26.9% higher than the company's current value. So, in the end, the BE stock could go up to $184.80.

Risks of the investment thesis

In spite of its advantages, this BE technology may encounter challenges that are not always within its control. This refers to external factors that cannot be managed. Firstly, the risk of changes to tax incentives in the US in 2026 should be taken into account, since this would make alternative energy sources less economical. Secondly, Bloom's capacity deployment is still sensitive to natural gas price volatility. That said, the efficiency of BE technology is around 60%, whereas traditional gas turbine power plants are around 35% to 40%. This means that BE is still less sensitive to sharp jumps in gas prices than its competitors in the traditional energy industry. Finally, the risk of execution is a factor, because Bloom is facing strong demand for its technology from data centers. For the company's management, therefore, their main task is to deploy new capacity to produce mini-power plants. This issue has been resolved in terms of financing, although it also depends on human resources, time, and available technology/equipment. Any delay by BE in scaling up its business could dampen the enthusiasm surrounding its shares.

Conclusion

Therefore, analysis of Bloom Energy's prospects reveals the company's unique competitive advantage, positioning it as a winner in the battle for data centers against inefficient public power grids. The main challenge, however, continues to be the deployment of additional capacity and scaling up production. The enormous potential of BE has yet to be realized. Taking these prospects and opportunities into account, I recommend a "Buy" rating for Bloom shares. It is not just the production of innovative power supply elements that I am betting on; it is the growth of the company, driven by the technological challenges and time constraints faced by traditional utility companies.

data.title: Flagstar Bank: Why We Initiated A New Position In Q4
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Third Avenue Small-Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

During the quarter, the Fund initiated a new position in Flagstar Bank, NA (FLG). Flagstar Bank, National Association (“Flagstar”) is a regional bank headquartered in New York and founded in 1859. Over the past few years, Flagstar has been undergoing a highly visible transformation featuring the legacy Flagstar Bank’s acquisition of New York Community Bank (2022) and the absorption of former Signature Bank assets purchased from the FDIC during the depths of the 2023 regional banking crisis. Fund management has been following the progress of Flagstar since 2023, with increased interest following a series of 2024 developments, including a greater than $1 billion capital infusion led by former Treasury Secretary Steve Mnuchin, through Liberty Strategic Capital, the sale of $5 billion of warehouse loans, divestment of its Mortgage Servicing division to Mr. Cooper and the appointment of Joe Otting as CEO and Chairman.

Combining the portfolio of New York Community Bank and Signature Bank resulted in an aggregation of portfolios with significant exposure to New York City rent-regulated multi-family properties. In recent years, changes to New York’s rent regulations have caused significant credit stress in the loan books of virtually all lenders historically active in this market. However, entering Q4 2025, Flagstar had made significant progress reducing its exposure to New York rent-regulated multi-family loans through asset sales, write-downs, restructuring and allowing maturing loans to be refinanced elsewhere. Progress shrinking this loan exposure has been impressive and is likely to continue.

Most recently, however, the New York City mayoral election of Zohran Mamdani has driven renewed fear of further onerous changes to rent regulations and depressed the valuation of Flagstar. The Fund has used this opportunity to initiate its position at a large discount to tangible book value, a seemingly attractive price for a bank that has taken large, aggressive write-downs on the challenged part of its legacy loan book, has been recapitalized, and has a credible path to achieve profitability more typical of a U.S. regional bank.

In the context of assessing Flagstar’s management team, we believe senior management has an unusually strong operating background demonstrated at previous banks, a credible plan to grow areas of lending other than commercial real estate, and exhibited a seriousness of purpose and skill as financiers. We perceive the company’s executive compensation to be structured in a way that creates attractive shareholder alignment and encourages resource conversion during a period of increasing consolidation in the U.S. regional banking industry. If Flagstar is successful in achieving its 2027 ambitions, it should go a long way towards shrinking the present discount to book value, while growing book value per share in the process.

Important Information

This publication does not constitute an offer or solicitation of any transaction in any securities. Any recommendation contained herein may not be suitable for all investors. Information contained in this publication has been obtained from sources we believe to be reliable, but cannot be guaranteed.

Also, please note that any discussion of the Fund’s holdings, the Fund’s performance, and the portfolio manager(s) views are as of December 31, 2025 (except as otherwise stated), and are subject to change without notice. Certain information contained in this letter constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe,” or the negatives thereof (such as “may not,” “should not,” “are not expected to,” etc.) or other variations thereon or comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results or the actual performance of any fund may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in any such forward-looking statement. Current performance results may be lower or higher than performance numbers quoted in certain letters to shareholders.

Date of first use of portfolio manager commentary: January 16, 2026

Past performance is no guarantee of future results; returns include reinvestment of all distributions. The above represents past performance and current performance may be lower or higher than performance quoted above. Investment return and principal value fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. For the most recent month-end performance please visit the Fund’s website at Third Avenue Management | Pioneers in Value Investing Since 1986. The gross expense ratio for the Fund’s Institutional, Investor and Z share classes is 1.25%, 1.51% and 1.18%, respectively, as of March 1, 2025.

Risks that could negatively impact returns include: fluctuations in currencies versus the US dollar, political/social/economic instability in foreign countries where the Fund invests, lack of diversification, volatility associated with investing in small-cap securities, and adverse general market conditions.

The fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses must be considered carefully before investing.
